We know that the total number of students in both dorms is ninety-six.
Five students moved from Maplewood to Oakwood recently.
After this move, the number of residents in both places became equal.
We need to find the original number of students in Oakwood.

Let us also define the initial count in Maplewood as another variable.
The sum of these two variables must equal ninety-six exactly.
When five students leave Maplewood, its count decreases by that amount.
When those same five students enter Oakwood, its count increases by that amount.
The new counts for both dormitories are now identical to each other.
We can set up an equation based on this equality condition.
The equation will help us solve for the unknown initial values.
Solving the math reveals that each dorm has forty-eight after the move.
Before the move, Oakwood had fewer students than Maplewood.
Specifically, Oakwood had forty-three students before the relocation occurred.
Maplewood originally had fifty-three students before any students moved.
If we add forty-three and fifty-three, we get the total of ninety-six.
Moving five from fifty-three leaves us with forty-eight students there.
Adding five to forty-three gives us forty-eight students in the other place.
The numbers match perfectly after the transfer is complete.
This confirms that the calculation is correct and logical.
